FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study Year 3 data (2019-2022) from US cohort of 10,048 adolescents aged 12-13 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone/SM variables: total recreational screen time (gaming, texting, social media, video chat, streaming); problematic social media use (SMAQ); problematic video game use (PVGQ); problematic mobile phone use (MPIQ)

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses with media parenting practices (6 categories) as predictors and screen time/problematic use outcomes. E.g., parental screen time modeling vs screen time: B=0.66, 95% CI 0.36-0.96

FT_IC4.evidence: β coefficients reported throughout: parental screen time modeling (B: 0.66, 95% CI: 0.36, 0.96 for screen time); family mealtime screen use (B: 1.24, 95% CI: 1.01, 1.47 for screen time); multiple others with p<0.05

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using ABCD Year 3 data with multiple linear regression

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Pediatric Research, 2025

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional design; media parenting practices are sociodemographic/contextual predictors but analyses are cross-sectional, not longitudinal (E7: cross-sectional any predictor → MET, FT-EC7 does not apply)
notes: All inclusion criteria met. Strong study with large diverse US ABCD sample, specific phone/SM variables, and reported coefficients with CIs.

FT_IC1.evidence: Review of Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study data of over 11,000 participants

FT_IC2.evidence: Reviews associations with specific SM variables: social media, video games, video chat, videos, texting

FT_IC3.evidence: Reviews cross-sectional and 1-2-year follow-up data showing associations between screen use and mental health outcomes

FT_IC4.evidence: No PDF available; abstract only. Cannot verify numeric coefficients reported

FT_IC5.evidence: This is a review article summarizing findings from ABCD Study, not original empirical analysis. No original analyses conducted by authors

FT_IC6.evidence: Published in Current Opinion in Pediatrics, 2025

FT_EC7_reason: Not applicable; FT-IC5 failed (review article, not original empirical work)
notes: EXCLUDED via FT-IC5 failure. This is a review/summary article, not original empirical research. Per prompt instructions, reviews fail FT-IC5.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study data from Period 2 follow-up (2018-2020) to Period 3 follow-up (2019-2021); N=9095 US adolescents aged 10-13 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Primary exposure is cyberbullying victimization (lifetime and past 12-month), not a smartphone/SM-specific variable. Total screen time included only as a covariate, not as the measured exposure or outcome. E6 applies: cyberbullying only, no separately-measured phone/SM variable as primary predictor or outcome

FT_IC3.evidence: Cannot assess; FT-IC2 failed. Primary analysis examines cyberbullying→manic symptoms, not phone/SM→outcome association

FT_IC4.evidence: Cannot assess; FT-IC2 failed. Coefficients reported are for cyberbullying (β=0.41, p=0.001) not phone/SM

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective/longitudinal analysis using multivariable linear regression with ABCD data

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Acta Paediatrica, 2025

FT_EC7_reason: Not applicable; FT-IC2 failed. Paper does not measure smartphone/SM as primary exposure or outcome variable
notes: EXCLUDED via FT-IC2 failure. Primary focus is cyberbullying victimization→manic symptoms association. While screen time is included as a covariate, no specific smartphone/SM variables are independently measured as exposure or outcome. Does not meet narrow smartphone/SM inclusion criteria.
